# GCAAI Unveils New Student Lounge at Gordon College
[Image: GCAAI Unveils New Student Lounge at Gordon College]
[Alumni](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/alumni/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 15, 2025**
GCAAI Unveils New Student Lounge at Gordon College
The Gordon College Alumni Association, Inc. (GCAAI) is proud to announce the official blessing and turnover of the new Gordon College Student Lounge. The lounge, a project lovingly referred to as the “Garden Project,” was blessed by Rev. Fr. Mike Duffin before being officially handed over to the college. This new space is for students, faculty, administration, alumni, and other members of the Gordon College community.
The initiative was spearheaded by Mr. Theo Roi N. Musni, GCAAI Public Information Officer, with the full support of Mr. Joseph Angelo B. Pusing, GCAAI President, the GCAAI Board of Trustees, GCAAI working committee members, the GC Board of Trustees headed by City Mayor Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., officials headed by College President Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, and all Gordon College alumni.
The blessing and turnover ceremony was attended by Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, Gordon College President, along with the college’s Vice Presidents. This new lounge stands as a testament to the GCAAI’s commitment to giving back to its alma mater.
Congratulations to the GCAAI and Gordon College!

# GC marks second year in One Billion Rising movement to end violence against women and girls
[Image: GC marks second year in One Billion Rising movement to end violence against women and girls]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, February 16, 2026**
Gordon College marked its second consecutive year of participation in the global campaign One Billion Rising through a public awareness activity themed “Rise for our bodies, our Earth, our Future,” held at Ayala Malls Harbor Point on February 14.  
The event brought together students and mall-goers in a collective call to stand against violence toward women and girls while promoting empowerment and social responsibility.  
Established in 2012, One Billion Rising has developed into an international movement recognized for mobilizing communities across the world to advocate for the elimination of gender-based violence.  
The initiative is grounded in global data from the United Nations indicating that approximately one in three women experience violence at some point in their lives, representing more than a billion women and girls worldwide.  
The activity was led by the Institutional Planning, Development, and External Affairs (IPDEA) through the Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit in partnership with the National Service Training Program (NSTP) and Ayala Malls Inc., with the goal of strengthening public awareness and encouraging community participation in the advocacy.  
One of the program’s highlights was a flash mob performance by Pase Ballo and SHAPES, who led a mass dance to the One Billion Rising anthem, Break the Chain, symbolizing unity and resistance.  
Mr. Joseph Atencio, head of the DEI Unit, emphasized that violence against women and girls does not only occur in public spaces but is often present within homes and personal relationships.  
“Bukod sa awareness na gusto nating ipakalat, gusto nating maging mapagmatyag ‘yung mga tao. The purpose of One Billion Rising is truly to end violence against women and girls. There can be no sustainable development without women’s empowerment,” he remarked. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara/The FOREFRONT
Photos by Ellisha Dawn & Angela Samson

• 𝐓𝐃𝐏 𝐁𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡 𝟕.𝟏 – 𝟐𝐧𝐝 𝐒𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫, 𝐀𝐜𝐚𝐝𝐞𝐦𝐢𝐜 𝐘𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟑–𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟒

c. Photocopy of the 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐈𝐃 (with signature) of the authorized representative, each with 𝐭𝐡𝐫𝐞𝐞 (𝟑) 𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐧 𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧𝐚𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐬.

# GC ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025
[Image: GC ALCUCOA Accreditation 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, November 26, 2025**
Nagsimula na ang tatlong-araw na akreditasyon ng Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) sa Gordon College.
Ngayong taon, ang Bachelor of Science in Business Administration (BSBA) Major in Marketing Management ay nangangarap makamit ang Level III Accreditation.
Samantala, ang Bachelor of Science in Accountancy (BSA), Bachelor in Elementary Education (BEEd), Bachelor of Early Childhood Education (BECEd), Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Filipino (BSEd-Fil), at Bachelor of Secondary Education Major in Science (BSEd-Sci) ay target naman ang Level II Accreditation.
Bukod dito, ang Bachelor of Culture and Arts Education (BCAEd) at Bachelor of Physical Education (BPEd) ay nominado bilang mga kandidato para sa akreditasyon ngayong taon.
Suportado ito ni Mayor [Atty. Rolen Paulino Jr.](https://www.facebook.com/mayorlenjpaulino?__cft__[0]=AZU3QvTqhQlkmJYMC3O-Ag6rBVcz3QYgae7wuVrFTGSN7kZ89KmJdl5ubPj8hN-KEPPY6EcfTQTGxYHZRibHJrQGqb402VnV70UrI9lSc8vH5vinQtVkACLAtBxyIbEXsw18OHsjlIkfBfyeqEXf7TUole-b6rZBCRPWdXyT96ESDoVWz3Fkm2GAvU73XCQkG6peAqxX1fWecL7WvNOwujSLGUFmxQTez5zZwVPmWci_DA&__tn__=-]K-y-R), na patuloy na tumatayong kaagapay ng Gordon College sa pagtataguyod ng kalidad na edukasyon para sa bawat Olongapeño.
Maraming salamat sa lahat ng accreditors; Library

# Ex-NSO prexy secures rank 9 spot; GC among top schools in Feb 2026 PNLE
[Image: Ex-NSO prexy secures rank 9 spot; GC among top schools in Feb 2026 PNLE]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, March 6, 2026**
Alumna Alexis Molon Penuliar of the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) ranked ninth in the February 2026 Philippine Nursing Licensure Examination (PNLE), as announced by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC) today, March 6.
Graduated cum laude, the former Gordon College – Nursing Student Organization (GC-NSO) president earned an 89.80% rating in the recent board examination.
The institution recorded 55 passers out of 60 total takers, posting an overall passing rate of 91.67%. Comprising this are 55 out of 57 first-time passers while none of the three repeat examinees passed.
Moreover, GC was among the country’s top-performing schools at 4th rank in the recent licensure exam with 50 to 99 examinees and at least 85% passing percentage, far surpassing the national passing rate of 44.24%.
Meanwhile, Alyanna Marie Ramos Villegas of Saint Paul University – Tuguegarao (SPU-T) was the February 2026 PNLE’s Top 1 Passer, having 92.00% rating. SPU-T also emerged as the Top 1 Performing School with 100 or more examinees, having 99.10% passing rate with 220 successful examinees out of 222.
PRC administered the examination on February 26 and 27 in various testing centers, including Manila, Baguio, Cebu, Davao, and several key provinces such as Palawan, Laguna, and Batangas. | via Alyssa Marie Alcantara and Keane Rafael Abdon/The FOREFRONT

# Proper Usage of Glass Boards
[Image: Proper Usage of Glass Boards]
**Thursday, May 23, 2024**
GUIDELINES FOR THE USE OF GLASS BOARD INSTALLED IN THE CLASSROOMS
* Use White Board Marker only to write on it
* Always erase marker ink from the surface after each use to prevent stains and ghosting.
* Don’t use dirty eraser or cloth, it will start spread ink residue across the surface of the board.
* Glass board must be cleared of any markings before the next class starts.
* Avoid using tapes or adhesives on the glass surface. These materials may be difficult and cause permanent damage to the glass board.
* Don’t use your hands to wipe a glass board! Your skin carries natural oils that over time leave a build-up of oil residue on the board. This can eventually ruin the surface of the glass board.
* Do not use sharp objects to avoid scratching or damaging the board surface.
* If the glass board is accidentally broken, it must be replaced with the same brand within seven (7) days from the date it was broken. If the item with specific brand and specification is not available, it must be replaced with three (3) timex of the quantity or in quantity comparable to the price of the item. The brand and specification must be approved first by the concerned personnel before replacement.

# LIMC History
Gordon College Library started in 1999 when the former Olongapo City Colleges, now Gordon College, put up a curricular program for Computer Studies and Business Management and Accountancy. With only a few books and mostly computer subjects in hand, Mrs. Remedios Sapuan, who was then a Utility worker, took charge of the library operation with the assistance of the Administrative Staff.
The Library moved four times to different locations in the building and its fourth was in the former KHG Hall. When the library was first opened, books were in the cabinet with glass doors and were locked. The library was using the close shelf system, where stacks cannot be directly accessed by users. The students should approach the assigned staff when borrowing books and it was then the bookshelf was opened to get the book needed.
In 2002, Thelma Ramos, a clerk aid of the Assessor’s Office, was transferred to the College Library to assist Mrs. Sapuan in the library operation. According to Mrs. Sapuan, a certain instructor, in the person of Mr. Sydney Maglalang, was temporarily assigned to the library. Services of the staff in the City Library were borrowed in the processing of books acquired for the College Library. In June 2003, the first College Librarian was hired, Ms. Maria Corazon D. Maneja as a casual position. Later she was promoted to a permanent position. The problem was by her was the book organization, the right classification of books, classification tools (DDC) Dewey Decimal Classification System and there were no catalog cards and cabinets. Some of the books were infested by termites which were already problems before but were not treated completely. Books were thenr eorganized, classified, and cataloged and were assigned new numbers using DD Classification tools that were borrowed from the Ci4ty Library. The circulation counter and grills dividing the books stacks and reading area were removed. Shelves were distributed and fastened on all library walls to make the books accessible to the users and to widen the reading area. Heavily termite-infested books were removed and deleted from the records. New books were formed. Newspapers were compiled with the date labeled and put on display. In 2006, books from Australia were received by the library through the Rotary Club of Olongapo. Books were found to be outdated and mostly for High School and Elementary level students. In the same year, another book donation was received from Asia Foundations, Inc. Books became very useful to patrons because of their general content. In March 2007, Ms. Betty Fielder, a friend of Mrs. Dely Aristorenas, former College Administrator, donated books and magazines for use by HRM students and faculty. Two air conditioners were installed to replace the former electric fans for ventilation and besides its small space, library patrons flocked the library especially nursing students who occupied the most number of enrollees. The library had then 8,361 volumes of books as of October 2007.
In the summer of 2010, the librarian during one of the EXECOM (ExecutiveCommittee) meetings, suggested removing over duplicated copies of books to provide spaces for new arrivals. Enough copies from weeded books were retained. 2,958 books were discarded and returned to GSO (Government Supply Office) but the latter suggested just donating them to the City Library.
In 2012, Gordon College's 13th Founding Anniversary, a Give-a-Book Project was launched and spearheaded by the College Library to collect tertiary level books from students, faculty, alumni, and friends of Gordon College. As of this date, May 6, 2013, we have collected 182 book donations.
On August 7, 2012, when the first knee-deep flood entered the College Library, books located on the first two-row level shelves were partially damaged but were restored. But on September 23, 2013, during a chest-deep heavy flood of Olongapo City, almost 2/3 of the library materials were soaked in the mud and water and devastated and discarded. Furniture which was made of light materials, files, and newly acquired books was included, but other computers were restored. Due to the kindness of City Mayor Rolen C. Paulino, he approved a 1 million budget for the purchase of new general education books, professional books, and journals. The first set of books was delivered in December 2013. Another era was opened for Gordon College Library for its transfer to the new building funded and built by SM in 2015-2016. The official transfer began on June 24, 2016, until July of the same year. The new and much wider library which is located on the fourth floor was opened to students on August 1, 2016, the start of the first semester, the Academic Year 2016- 2017.
Mr. Marlon A. Atencio recommended to the Executive Council the change of the library name into “Library and Instructional Media Center” for the reason that the library is now having a media center that supports the educational needs of the library and it was approved by the Executive Council spearheaded by the College President, Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano last May 31, 2018.

# Avoid Cellphone Use on Stairs
[Image: Avoid Cellphone Use on Stairs]
**Friday, April 17, 2026**
**Safety Reminder Regarding Cellphone Use on Stairways**  
To ensure the safety of all students and faculty, we are reminding everyone of the following guideline regarding electronic device usage on stairs:
**Policy:**  
Cellphone use is strictly prohibited while ascending or descending staircases. This includes texting, browsing social media, making calls, or playing games.
**Rationale:**  
Stairways are high-traffic areas that require full attention. Using a cellphone while walking significantly reduces spatial awareness and reaction time, increasing the risk of:
* Slips, trips and falls
* Collisions with other students
* Blocking the flow of traffic during passing periods
**Procedure:**
* **Pause:** If you must send a message or answer a call, step to the side and stop walking before using your device.
* **Pocket:** Keep phones stored in pockets or bags while navigating steps.
* **Proceed:** Only resume walking once the device is put away and your full attention is on the stairs.
Thank you for helping us keep our campus safe.

# CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles
[Image: CHTM Sharks sealed the set against CEAS Mentors in Men’s Singles]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
TRICKSHOT COVERED COURT—The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks bagged the gold again during the men’s badminton singles championship match, 21-6, defeating the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors.
With his outstanding performance, Kryztofer Matias Basa of CHTM Sharks ended the finals with two straight sets, leaving no chance for his opponent to recover.
In an interview, Basa shared that he already expected the victory as he had once played with a stronger player before.
“‘Yu‘n pa yung ginamit ko na motivation para magchampion ulit yung CHTM,” he said when asked about being pressured to defend the previous win of CHTM last SIKLAB.
He also expressed his joy in playing with passion and humility. “Iniisip ko lang is maging humble, and…sportsmanship,” he added.
Moreover, the College of Computer Studies secured the bronze medal, while the College of Business and Accountancy and the College of Allied Health got the 4th and 5th place, respectively. | via Jeliza Tadena/The FOREFRONT

# HON. ROLEN C. PAULINO JR. RECEIVES THE MODEL LOCAL CHIEF EXECUTIVE AWARD FOR 2025 FROM ALCUCOA
[Image: HON. ROLEN C. PAULINO JR. RECEIVES THE MODEL LOCAL CHIEF EXECUTIVE AWARD FOR 2025 FROM ALCUCOA]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, December 9, 2025**
Huge congratulations to our visionary City Mayor, Hon. Rolen C. Paulino Jr., who was conferred the prestigious Model Local Chief Executive Award for 2025 by the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) on December 9, 2025, at the Diamond Hotel in Manila.
Our City Mayor received this top honor in recognition of his exemplary leadership and outstanding commitment to advancing quality and accessible higher education in Olongapo. His administration was specifically lauded for successfully elevating academic standards and modernizing campus infrastructure to secure a globally competitive educational landscape for the city’s constituents.

# THE FOREFRONT WINS BIG AT 5TH AOCPA
[Image: THE FOREFRONT WINS BIG AT 5TH AOCPA]
[ForeFront](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/forefront/) [OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, February 13, 2025**
SAN FERNANDO, Pampanga – The Forefront, the official student publication of Gordon College, garnered five awards at the 5th Assumption Outstanding Campus Press Awards (AOCPA) held at the University of the Assumption on January 27, 2025.
Thirty publications across Luzon participated in the competition, submitting a total of 278 entries according to Regina, the official student publication of the University of the Assumption.
The Forefront’s award-winning staff members are:
Patrisha Marie Pineda – 4th Most Outstanding Investigative Journalist
John Carlo Bermudez – 8th Most Outstanding Literary Writer
Stephanie Estrella – 8th Most Outstanding Opinion Writer
Lance Isaac Leon – 9th Most Outstanding Editorial Cartoonist
John Patrick Mateo – 10th Most Outstanding Feature Writer
The conference is an annual iteration of UA’s Regina in search for the most outstanding campus journalists in different categories across Luzon.

# GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025
[Image: GC marks a milestone in ALCUCOA 2025]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, December 3, 2025**
The three-day program comes to an end as Gordon College (GC) conducts an exit conference for the Association of Local Colleges and Universities Commission on Accreditation (ALCUCOA) last November 28, at the Gordon College Main Building, Function Hall.
Vying for one Level III program, five Level II programs, and two candidate programs this year, the institution’s big step is a leap for development, embodying character, excellence, and service along the way.
“This is not the end,” Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano opened the exit conference of this year’s accreditation with a firm statement.
The accreditors commended the institution’s strengths including the Library Ambassadors Organization (LAO), community extension programs, and professionalism.
Meanwhile, they also recommended improvements such as stronger programs to supplement both students’ and teachers’ needs, more tangible and sustainable community extensions, and subjects that focus on application rather than theories.
As Dr. Elizabeth Montero, the supervising accreditor said in her message, “The new oil now is consistency,” a reminder to stay true and stand into the lenses of continuous development where growth is evident and seen.
In response, Ms. Geraldine Raydanas and Mrs. Rowenalyn Sagun expressed their utmost gratitude and promised to improve in the areas mentioned.
Ending the program, Dr. Kristin Joy Mendoza, secretary of GC’s Board of Trustees, left a valuable statement regarding institutionalization and sustainability.
“Every accreditation is an opportunity to innovate and fill the spaces where we can do better,” Dr. Mendoza emphasized.
Meanwhile, the results of the 2025 accreditation are set to be announced in the next few weeks. | via Mikhaela Sagun/The FOREFRONT

# CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games
[Image: CCS Phoenix Clinches Gold at SIKLAB 2025 E-Games]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, September 17, 2025**
FUNCTION HALL, GORDON COLLEGE MAIN BLDG.—The Gordon College Main Building Function Hall buzzed with excitement as SIKLAB 2025 E-Games came to a spectacular close, featuring intense battles for gold, silver, and bronze.
In the Battle for Bronze, the College of Education, Arts, and Science (CEAS) Mentors defeated the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) Tigers, 22-8, securing third place and bringing pride to the CEAS community.
Meanwhile, the championship series between the CCS Phoenix and the CHTM Sharks was a nail-biter in a best-of-three format. CCS edged CHTM in Game 1, 21-20, followed by a 17-win in Game 2. In the final showdown, the Phoenix dominated, 12-1, clinching the gold medal with an overall 2-1 victory.
The CHTM Sharks earned the silver medal, demonstrating remarkable perseverance and strategy throughout the finals.
SIKLAB 2025 E-Games concluded as a remarkable showcase of determination, strategy, and camaraderie, crowning the CCS Phoenix as champions while bringing the excitement of competitive E-sports to Gordon College—and leaving fans eagerly anticipating next year’s edition. | via Frances Marie Farne/The FOREFRONT

# TF SECURES LHEPC SPOT
[Image: TF SECURES LHEPC SPOT]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, February 14, 2026**
The Forefront journos once again demonstrated their grit and skills in campus journalism, securing several awards at the 24th Regional Higher Education Press Conference (RHEPC), held at Palm Cabanas Resort, Arayat, Pampanga, from February 11 to 13.
Two of the publication’s outgoing executive members, John Patrick Mateo (Editor-in-Chief) and Lance Isaac Leon (Associate Editor), were recognized as Outstanding Tertiary Campus Journalists (OTCJ) in Region III.
In the group category, the pub’s entry for Opening Billboard ranked 4th Place overall.
In individual categories, delegates from the publication bagged nine (9) awards as listed below.
Angelique Jose
1st Place – Traditional Literary Graphics Illustration (English)
Lance Isaac Leon
2nd Place – Editorial Writing (English)
3rd Place – Opinion Writing (English)
John Patrick Mateo
3rd Place – Devcom Writing (Filipino)
John Christian Rocero
3rd Place – Photojournalism (English)
Mary Isobelle Arcilla
5th Place – Traditional Comic Strip Drawing (English)
6th Place – Traditional Editorial Cartooning (English)
Keane Rafael Abdon
8th Place – News Writing (English)
Mikhaela Sagun
10th Place – Poetry Writing (English)
Overall, The Forefront is the 11th Highest Pointer in individual categories against 42 participating college publications in Central Luzon.
Additionally, Keane Rafael Abdon is the newly elected division coordinator of Olongapo City for the Young Journalist Association of Region III (YJAR-III).
Five campus journalists from the publication, who garnered top 5 placements in their respective categories, will advance to the 21st Luzon-Wide Higher Education Press Conference (LHEPC) in Tuguegarao City, Cagayan, next month to compete in eight categories. | via Mikhaela Sagun and Lance Isaac Leon, THE FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson
[Image: Gordon College Welcomes IYES Language School for Cultural Exchange and Nihongo Lesson]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, March 9, 2026**
Last March 6, 2026, Gordon College, through its International Affairs and Linkages Office, welcomed visitors from IYES Language School. Two Japanese students conducted an engaging educational and cultural exchange with 2nd Year BS Tourism Management students taking THP101 – Foreign Language 1 under Ms. Jacqueline Fabian, Instructor. The class visit featured a mini cultural presentation and an introductory Japanese (Nihongo) lesson, where the visiting students shared insights into Japanese culture, including common conversations and greetings, as well as interesting Japanese onomatopoeic expressions used in daily life. The highlight of the session was the interactive Q&A activity, where students enthusiastically exchanged questions and ideas, creating a lively and meaningful dialogue between cultures. This initiative aimed to foster cross-cultural understanding, provide opportunities for both visitors and students to share experiences and perspectives, and create a warm, interactive, and memorable learning experience for everyone involved.

# TF’s Luto, among top newsletters at 2026 AOCPA; scribes bag 4 individual awards
[Image: TF’s Luto, among top newsletters at 2026 AOCPA; scribes bag 4 individual awards]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, March 7, 2026**
he Forefront, the official student publication of Gordon College, took home the 8th Most Outstanding Campus Newsletter and four individual awards at the 6th Assumption Outstanding Campus Press Awards (AOCPA).
The event, hosted by the University of the Assumption’s Regina, took place in San Fernando, Pampanga, on February 27.
The first semestral issue of The Forefront, Luto, was one of this year’s recipients of Most Outstanding Campus Newsletter.
During the awards ceremony, the publication’s Editor-in-Chief John Patrick Mateo earned the 2nd Most Outstanding Campus Journalist award.
Opinion-Editorial Editor Stephanie Estrella’s piece, “Pundido ang ilaw sa susunod na kalsada”, made her the 4th Most Outstanding Editorial Writer and Punto Central Luzon’s Choice for Most Outstanding Editorial Writer.
Meanwhile, Head of Visual Arts Maryisobelle Arcilla was named the 10th Most Outstanding Editorial Cartoonist in another Luzon-wide stint.
The annual awards, featuring 46 publications from various regions in the north, saw The Forefront emerge as one of Luzon’s best.

# CEAS Women’s Historic Smash Hit: Another Gold in Futsal Match
[Image: CEAS Women’s Historic Smash Hit: Another Gold in Futsal Match]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, September 19, 2025**
What a thrilling clash between pink and blue.
The College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors defeated College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks after a long showdown that ended in 3-0, and finished as champions in the Women’s Futsal match on the last day of SIKLAB 2025, held at New Asinan covered court.
The Mentors did not let their guard down ever since their first game, making sure everything is coordinated to one another until the final match.
Cheers roared through the court as the intensifying moments of the game went on, with Mentors maintaining their momentum throughout the match.
Despite the Sharks’ efforts to lessen the pressure, CEAS women clocked the game with a winning score of 3-0. | via Mariah Llave/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Named Top 4 Performing School in Feb 2026 NLE with 91.67% Passing Rate
[Image: Gordon College Named Top 4 Performing School in Feb 2026 NLE with 91.67% Passing Rate]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Saturday, March 7, 2026**
Huge congratulations to the College of Allied Health Studies (CAHS) for its outstanding performance in the recently conducted Nursing Licensure Examination (NLE) held on February 26–27, 2026. Gordon College has officially secured its spot as the TOP 4 PERFORMING SCHOOL with a remarkable institutional passing rate of 91.67%. This achievement is particularly exceptional as it more than doubles the National Passing Rate of 44.24%, proving once again that our educational standards are truly world-class. Alongside this institutional success, we celebrate the individual brilliance of Ms. Alexis Molon Penuliar, RN, for clinching the TOP 9 spot with a stellar rating of 89.80%.
To all our newly Registered Nurses, we are incredibly proud of your hard work and dedication!
This milestone is a testament to the unwavering support and visionary leadership of Hon. Rolen C. Paulino, Jr., City Mayor; Hon. Cristabelle Marie C. Paulino, Chairperson of the Committee on Education; Dr. Imelda DP. Soriano, College President; and our dedicated academic leaders: Dr. Darwin P. Paguio (VPAA), Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr. (CAHS Dean), Ms. Sheila Marie P. Oconer (CAHS Assistant Dean), and Ms. April Joyce Liban-Dela Cruz (BSN Program Chair). # “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25
[Image: “2,000 change-makers” attend NSTP grad ‘25]
[NSTP](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/nstp/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, June 8, 2025**
Two thousand Gordon College (GC) students enrolled in their National Service and Training Program (NSTP) courses filled the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City, on Wednesday, for this year’s NSTP Graduation Ceremony.
With the theme, “Volunteerism in Action: Shaping Leaders, Strengthening Community”, this year’s batch of NSTP graduates was graced by Miss Manila 2014 Dr. Krischelle D. Halili.
Dr. Halili emphasized that leadership is an “act not for yourself, but for others” in her address. “Serve something greater than yourself and take your friends with you.”
Moreover, she called on students to love the country and God while also channeling ‘tech natives’ to participate in online community initiatives as well as community services and university council activities on-site.
Leading the initial confirmation of the graduates, Vice President for Academic Affairs Dr. Darwin P. Paguio also delivered his special message, focusing on the impact of genuine leadership.
“True leadership is not defined merely by title or accolades but by the impact you make in the lives of others,” he said as he congratulated the “two thousand change-makers” afterward.
This was followed by the awarding of certificates to Literary Training Service (LTS) and Civic Welfare Training Service (CWTS) completers from the different departments of the institution.
NSTP Instructor Mr. Hubert Lew Magrata delivered his closing remarks to officially close the program.

# Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College
[Image: Bagong Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan, pinasinayaan sa Gordon College]
[CEAS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/ceas/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Sunday, September 7, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE—Inukit na ang mga bagong lakan at lakambini ng kasaysayan kasabay ng panapos na selebrasyon ng Buwan ng Kasaysayan 2025 na may temang “Diwa ng Kasaysayan, Kabilin sa Kabataan” na idinaos noong Agosto 30, sa Gordon College Physical Education (PE) Hall sa pangunguna ng Samahan ng mga Mag-aaral sa Araling Panlipunan (SMAP).
Nagwagi sina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales mula sa BSEd Soc 3-A at Bb. Angel Mae Deseo mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A para sa Lakan at Lakambini ng Kasaysayan 2025.
Nasungkit naman nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A ang korona bilang Ginoong Unang Gantimpala at Binibining Unang Gantimpala.
Namayagpag din bilang Ginoong Ikalawang Gantimpala at Binibining Ikalawang Gantimpala sina G. Rafael Ellado mula sa BSEd Soc 1-A at Bb. Gervy Sarmiento mula sa BSEd Soc 2-A.
Bukod sa Lakan at Lakambini 2025, iginawad din ang mga espesyal na parangal tulad ng Lunting Diwa at Best in Talent na parehong nakuha nina G. Jhan Girbaud Hipolito at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho.
Nakamit naman nina G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales at Bb. Jhade Aizelle Quiocho ang mga titulong Best in Formal Attire at People’s Choice Award.
Samantala, inuwi naman nina Bb. Angel Mae Deseo at G. Lorenzen Rob Gonzales ang karangalang Binibini at Ginoo ng Nakaraan.
“Nung sinabi na ako yung panalo, naging proud ako kasi nagbunga yung suporta ng pamilya at mga kaibigan, higit sa lahat, nagbunga yung effort at tiwala ko sa sarili” saad ng Lakan 2025.
“In-expect ko talaga na may chance akong manalo.” Ibinahagi din ni Lorenzen na pinaghirapan niya ang kanyang preparasyon, mula ensayo hanggang pagpapakilala at pagsagot sa tanong ng mga hurado.
Samantala, labis naman ang pasasalamat ni Bb. Angel Mae Deseo, Lakambini 2025, sa lahat ng tumulong sa kanya lalo na at noong panahong iyon ay masama pa ang kanyang karamdaman.
“Tinrangkaso po ako hanggang sa mismong laban, sa kabila nun ay ipinagpatuloy ko ang laban sapagkat nakikita ko ang aking mga kamag-aral (SOC 1-A) na nagtutulong-tulong upang matapos ang lahat ng aming kakailanganin,” saad ni Angel Mae Deseo.
“Lahat ng pagod at puyat ay naging makabuluhan sapagkat nasungkit namin ang korona at karangalan,” aniya. | via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

# Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings
[Image: Gordon College Joins the 2025 Times Higher Education Impact Rankings]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, June 18, 2025**
We are proud to announce that Gordon College has officially earned a place in the prestigious Times Higher Education (THE) Impact Rankings 2025! Despite being a newcomer to the global stage, GC was ranked among the top 1,500+ out of 2,526 higher education institutions (HEIs) from 130 countries, assessed for their contributions to achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs)—a recognition based on universities’ social impact through research, stewardship, outreach, and teaching.
Gordon College participated in four SDG categories and earned the following standings:
• SDG 3 – Good Health and Well-being: Ranked 1001–1500
• SDG 4 – Quality Education: Ranked 1501+
• SDG 10 – Reduced Inequalities: Ranked 1001+
• SDG 17 – Partnerships for the Goals: Ranked 1501+
More than just recognition, this milestone reflects our evolving vision—to become a globally recognized local institution making meaningful contributions to global progress through inclusive, equitable, and sustainable development in higher education.

# Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education
[Image: Gordon College Faculty Join Basic FSL Training to Promote Inclusive Education]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Friday, August 29, 2025**
Faculty members from the College of Education, Arts and Sciences (CEAS) and the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) with Mr. Joseph Atencio-OIC, Head of Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) Unit proudly took part in a three-day Basic Filipino Sign Language (FSL) Training held last August 26–28, 2025 at the SMX Convention Center, Olongapo City. The event was organized by the Olongapo City Persons with Disability Affairs Office (PDAO) in collaboration with the Department of Education – Olongapo City Division Office.
Throughout the training, participants engaged in interactive and hands-on activities designed to equip educators with the skills to communicate effectively with the Deaf—nurturing a more inclusive, and compassionate learning environment.
This initiative not only strengthened our instructors’ professional competencies but also reaffirmed their dedication to accessibility, diversity, and inclusivity in education—a true reflection of Gordon College’s commitment to ensuring that no learner is left behind.

# ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes
[Image: ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes]
**Monday, January 19, 2026**
**ANNOUNCEMENT:**
**ONLINE Adding/Dropping of Classes**
*Second Semester Academic Year 2025-2026*
**January 19-30, 2026**
[Image: ☑️]
Step 1
Log-on to the student portal ([https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student](https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gces/student?fbclid=IwZXh0bgNhZW0CMTAAYnJpZBExZWpzSlY3MHlQdVJWaXA0bnNydGMGYXBwX2lkEDIyMjAzOTE3ODgyMDA4OTIAAR5DCtJJZeE0IeMkAlOWYkAjlJ5HTa_uyuxL5M64lTq3dzJieHkPXJaQ0YalBg_aem_XfOZPmWrrx4p7jcYpDLqDg))
Click Add/Drop Request Button under Menu Items.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 2
To request for Dropping of classes, under Current Schedule section, click Drop button.
To request for adding of classes, under Add Subjects section, type the detail of the class (you can search by class code, subject or by description) on the search box. Click Add button of the desired class.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 3
Your selected subject(s) will appear under List of Subjects to Add/Drop section. Repeat step 2 to add or drop more classes.
Click submit to finalize your request or cancel to clear selected classes.
[Image: ☑️]
Step 4
Your request will be evaluated by your program coordinator and will be verified by the college registrar.
Regularly visit the GCES portal for request updates.

# BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting
[Image: BASURA, PASILIDAD, UNIPORME: Students Address Concerns In Town Hall Meeting]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Tuesday, November 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE FUNCTION HALL MAIN BLDG—In an effort to strengthen transparency and communication between the student body and the administration, various students and representatives from different organizations voice out their concerns and proposed solutions on pressing campus issues that affect their daily experiences on the Town Hall Meeting, spearheaded by the Supreme Student Council (SSC) of Gordon College (GC) on November 4, at Gordon College Function Hall Main Bldg.
Among the major issues discussed were concerns with the disappearance of trash bins, restroom malfunctions, and the absence of student lounges, which reflect the everyday struggles of GCians that often go unnoticed. While these may seem like small matters, they collectively define the comfort, cleanliness, and morale of the student body.
Meanwhile, other pressing matters, such as the condition of the main and annex buildings and inconsistent implementation of the dress code, point to deeper issues of transparency, inclusivity, and student welfare.
DISAPPEARANCE OF TRASH BINS: CLEANLINESS AND WASTE MANAGEMENT ISSUES RAISED
Campus cleanliness had been a pressing issue among students following the recent disappearance of trash bins across campus. Students reported that many areas, including restrooms, now lack accessible waste bins, leading to increased littering and cleanliness issues.
Participants suggest restoration of the trash bins throughout the campus to promote cleanliness and for students’ easier access, emphasizing that this could help maintain a clean environment around the campus.
BUILDING FUNCTIONALITY: CALLS FOR BETTER FACILITIES BOTH IN THE MAIN AND ANNEX BUILDINGS
Moreover, the Annex Building, which accommodates a growing number of classes, points to another major concern. Students described poor ventilation, unstable internet connectivity, and a lack of proper equipment—including the sound system in the Function Room—as recurring problems that lessen their productivity.
Furthermore, students also expressed frustrations over the lack of equipment in the Main Building, such as practicums and simulations.
The SSC noted the importance of these concerns and emphasized the need for facility upgrades to ensure a more conducive academic environment, emphasizing that as Gordon College aims to be a university, infrastructure and facility improvements must keep pace with academic demands.
DRESS CODE VIOLATIONS: STUDENTS CALL FOR CONSISTENCY AND CLARITY
One of the most discussed topics was the implementation of the college’s dress code policy. Several students expressed confusion over inconsistent enforcement across departments, citing instances of inconsistency and a lack of clear communication throughout the student body.
“Minsan po navi-violation kami kahit appropriate po ‘yung suot namin [na uniform],” one Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Student expressed.
Participants also noted that some students are reprimanded for minor violations while others are not held to the same standard.
“Naka-org shirt po ako then ni-question po ako kung bakit [naka-org shirt]. Nag-explain po ako na may event, pero may nauna po sakin na naka-civilian [pero] ‘di po siya ni-question,” another student from College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) shared.
In the latter part of the meeting, Gcians requested clearer guidelines and a more uniform implementation across departments to help promote both discipline and inclusivity within the campus community, and avoid misunderstanding and unequal treatment.
STUDENT LOUNGE, RESTROOM, AND OTHER CONCERNS
The functionality and cleanliness of restrooms were also brought up as a long-standing concern. Some facilities were reported to have malfunctioning faucets and dysfunctional doors, toilets, and bidets. Students cite concerns for regular maintenance checks to ensure comfort and hygiene for all.
On the other hand, Participants also voiced out frustrations over the lack of student lounges and rest areas where they can relax, study, or collaborate during vacant periods. Many pointed out that the few available classrooms require permission to be used, forcing students to stay in hallways or stairwells in between classes.
One student from CHTM proposed designating student lounges on each floor to provide space for students as a resolution, citing that this would provide comfort for students.
The SSC assured attendees that all issues and recommendations raised will be compiled and formally addressed to the college administration for further deliberation with the BIBINGKA project, held on November 7, Friday.
The council also promised its commitment to continue facilitating such dialogues to ensure that students’ voices are not only heard but also acted upon. | via Love Lorraine Acedo/The FOREFRONT

Soriano; and our dedicated academic leaders: Dr. Darwin P. Paguio (VPAA), Dr. Victor C. Quimen Jr., Ms. Sheila Marie P. Oconer, and Ms. April Joyce Liban-Dela Cruz.

# Tulong Dunong Program (TDP) and Tertiary Education Subsidy (TES) Distribution
[Image: Tulong Dunong Program (TDP) and Tertiary Education Subsidy (TES) Distribution]
**Friday, February 20, 2026**
[Image: 📢]
ANNOUNCEMENT
Tulong Dunong Program (TDP) and Tertiary Education Subsidy (TES) Distribution
To all listed TDP and TES grantees who were unable to claim their financial grants on the scheduled payout, you are hereby given a final claiming schedule as follows:
[Image: 📅]
Date: February 24, 2026 (Tuesday)
[Image: ⏰]
Time: 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM
[Image: 📍]
Venue: Room 415, Gordon College – Main Campus
This payout covers the following:
• TDP Batch 3 – 2nd Semester, Academic Year 2024–2025
• TES Listahanan 15 – 2nd Semester, Academic Year 2024–2025
[Image: 📄]
Required Documents
Please bring the following complete requirements:
1. Three (3) photocopies of your student ID or valid government ID (with signature), each with three (3) specimen signatures.
2. For Authorized Representatives (Immediate Family ONLY) — submit all in three (3) copies:
a. Original Authorization Letter with the grantee’s wet signature.
b. Photocopy of the student ID or valid government ID (with signature) of the grantee, each with three (3) specimen signatures.
c. Photocopy of the valid government ID (with signature) of the authorized representative, each with three (3) specimen signatures.
3. TES Grantees ONLY
• Bring your Narrative Report.
[Image: 🔔]
Important Notes
• Only those who were unable to attend the previous payout schedule may claim during this period.
• Incomplete documents will not be processed.
• This is NOT for application.
Please be guided accordingly.

# CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025
[Image: CHTM Sharks Roar at 2nd CHTM Rally 2025]
[CHTM](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/chtm/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Thursday, September 11, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE — The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) filled the East Tapinac Covered Court with cheers and chants as the 2nd CHTM Rally unfolded last September 9, 2025, igniting the spirit of the CHTM Sharks in preparation for the much-anticipated Siklab 2025 Sportsfest on September 15–19, 2025.
The event showcased the final lineup of the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) official teams and players.
Furthermore, athletes from the CHTM Sharks introduced themselves through lively intermission numbers performed by each sports team, adding excitement and entertainment to the rally.
Player representatives from each team also participated in a brief interview with the Masters of Ceremony, where they shared their thoughts and enthusiasm for the upcoming Siklab competition.
Moreover, the event was organized in collaboration with the College of Hospitality and Tourism Management Student Council (CHTM-SC), Arapaap Productions, the Junior Association of Hospitality Management Students (JAHMS), and the League of Tourism Students of the Philippines (LTSP), who worked together to ensure the program’s success.
The College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Dean, Dr. Mark Alvin Lazaro, Assistant Dean Vincent Dela Cruz, Bachelor of Science and Tourism Management (BSTM) Program Coordinator Ma’am Lizette Ann P. Sia, Bachelor of Science in Hospitality Management (BSHM) Coordinator Vince Richard Gomez, and ARAPAAP Adviser Ms. Jenny Mae M. Lagman also extended strong support and leadership, which greatly guided the preparations for the rally.
To cap off the event, the atmosphere reached its peak as the CHTM Sharks filled the covered court with their department’s official Siklab cheer, embodying their spirit of unity and readiness for the upcoming sportsfest.| via Lovely Doroin/The FOREFRONT

GORDON COLLEGE — As the competition arises, five- contestants received a triumphant win to special awards.
On a two-sets of awarding, Mx. Albert Panotes, Mx. College of Health and Allied Studies (CAHS) received the Mx. Congeniality, Mx. Beautiful Body, Best in Pride Attire, and Best in Formal Wear.

And to end the night, Joshua Salgado, Mx. College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) is awarded with Mx. Healthy Smile and Mx. Perfect Skin.

# GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers
[Image: GC Ranks 3 in May 2025 PNLE; 2 Alumni Among Topnotchers]
[CAHS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/cahs/) [News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Wednesday, May 14, 2025**
Gordon College (GC) secures third spot among top performing schools in the May 2025 Philippine Nurses Licensure Examination (PNLE), earning a rating of 98.29%, as 115 out of 117 examinees successfully made the cut.
Moreover, two GC Alumni; Martin Jorge Bernal Alcantara and Loren Mirador Mistica, emerged as top 9, both acing a rate of 88.20%.
This milestone not only underscores the college’s commitment to academic excellence but also reinforces its standing as a premier institution for nursing education in the country.

# CHTM Sharks, sinaliwan ang ritmo tungong panalo
[Image: CHTM Sharks, sinaliwan ang ritmo tungong panalo]
[News](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/news/)
**Monday, September 15, 2025**
GORDON COLLEGE, OLONGAPO CITY—Nagliyab sa entablado ang College of Hospitality and Tourism Management (CHTM) Sharks sa Dancesports Competition—Latin Category nang ipamalas ang kanilang husay sa Cha-cha na sumubok sa indayog ng mga ritmo, Rumba na nagbigay-diin sa senswalidad, at Jive na nagpaigting ng sigla at enerhiya ng SIKLAB 2025.
Matapos ang tatlong antas ng laban, nangibabaw sina John Michael Apan at Cathlyn Heart Del Rosario ng CHTM Sharks. Ipinamalas nila ang matinding dedikasyon, tiyaga, at koordinasyon sa bawat hakbang at bawat himig ng musika. Tinanghal silang kampeon ng gabi sa kategoryang Latin.
Sinundan naman ito ng College of Education, Arts, and Sciences (CEAS) Mentors na sumungkit sa ikalawang pwesto at College of Business and Accountancy (CBA) Wizards sa ikatlong pwesto.
“Nagpapasalamat po ako sa Diyos sa guidance, also sa lakas. Unexpected po yung nangyari, aminado naman po kami na lahat ng department malalakas, also dahil nga po last year hindi namin nakuha ‘yong gold. Mas pinag-igihan na lang po namin yung training namin. Sobrang saya po namin na champion po kami,” ani ni John Michael, sa naramdamang galak matapos magwagi.
Dagdag pa ni Cathlyn, natural lang sa mag-partner ang mag-away. Hindi man daw nagkakasundo sa gustong steps; mga routines, mga gustong minsa’y hindi nauunawan. Ngunit, nagagawan naman ng paraan at naaayos sa usapan.
Hindi lamang sa tropeyo at ranggo umikot ang laban. Sa huli, ipinakita ng Latin Category ang sigasig at diwa ng komunidad ng Gordon College—kung saan ang bawat indak ay larawan ng determinasyon, pagkakaisa, at pagmamahal sa sining ng sayaw ng Gordon College Community. | via Lianne Malazarte/The FOREFRONT

# Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)
[Image: Online Application for GC Admission Test Procedure (1st Semester, A.Y. 2024-2025)]
[OSWS](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/osws/)
**Monday, December 18, 2023**
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 1. CREATE ONLINE PORTAL ACCOUNT**
1.1 Go to Gordon College Admission Portal login page (<https://gordoncollegeccs.edu.ph/gca>)
1.2 Click “Create Account”
1.3 Type your email address, given name, last name, date of birth, contact number, desired password and select type of student (New Student, Transferee, Second Courser)
1.4 Click “Register”
Notes:
o A valid and existing email address is required in this process.
o Do not create multiple accounts as this will be a ground for disapproval of application.
· Do not forget your email and password
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 2. LOG ON TO YOUR ACCOUNT**
2.1 Go to the portal login page
2.2 Type your previously registered email address and password then click “Log In”
2.3 You will be directed to the portal Dashboard.
2.4 The Dashboard displays the status of your application.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 3. PROFILING**
3.1 On the upper left side of the page, click “Upload Photo” to upload your recent photo.
Guide:
o 2×2 colored picture, white background
o Formal pose wearing collared top, No eyeglasses or any accessories like earrings.
o Picture should be taken not later than one week prior to filing of application.
3.2 On the Menu, click “Profile”
3.3 Completely and accurately fill out the forms (Personal Information, Family Background and Desired Programs)
3.4 Read the Gordon College General Data Privacy Notice and click the CHECKBOX.
3.5 Review the form and click “Submit”
**[Image: 🟢]
STEP 4. UPLOADING OF DOCUMENTS**
4.1 On the Menu, click “Uploads”
4.2 Upload scanned or clear image of the required documents. Click the placeholder to browse for an image to upload.
4.3 The image file should be less than 2MB to avoid uploading errors
Required documents:
o Recent Good Moral Character Certificate
o PSA Authenticated Birth Certificate
o Senior High School ID or Any Valid ID Card
– Click “Submit” to finalize the uploading.
[Image: 🟢]
 **STEP 5. GCAT Schedule**
5.1 The schedule of GCAT will be posted to your portal account within three (3) working days after submission of COMPLETE application and required documents.
5.2 Upon posting of the test schedule on the online portal, you may download and print the following:
o GCAT Schedule slip
o Student Information Sheet
————————————————–
What to bring on the test day?
1. GCAT Schedule Slip
2. Student Information Sheets
3. Senior High School or any valid ID Card
5. Pencil and Pen
Note: Please be informed that all applications shall be submitted online to prevent the spread of Covid-19.

# Rights and Obligations of GCAAI Members
#### Rights of Members
All members, regular and honorary, who complied with all the requirements, guidelines, and policies set forth by the Association are members in good standing.
Every member in good standing shall have the following rights:
1. To attend the regular and special meetings of the general membership of the Association;
2. To vote and be elected into office;
3. To propose amendments and resolutions, and to vote on those presented at the meetings above mentioned;
4. To request access to official records, documents, and papers pertaining to the acts, transactions, or decisions of the Association unless otherwise specified;
5. To use the properties and facilities of the Association according to the rules and regulations agreed upon for this purpose;
6. Such other rights and privileges as may be granted by the Board of Trustees.
#### Article 4 Section 5 – The right to vote and be elected as Trustees
The right to vote and be elected as Trustees of this Association shall be exclusively granted to Active Members and members in good standing. Provided, they are listed in the SEC Membership Book/and other forms of registration conducted by the Association and shall be certified correct by the Corporate Secretary. Provided, further, that only active members shall be appointed to any position in the Association.
#### Article 4 Section 6
It is understood that those who have been accepted members of the Association prior to the effectivity of the present By-Laws shall continue to be active members. Provided, further, that after the first general assembly and election, they shall satisfy the requirements stipulated under Section 3.
#### Duties and Obligations of Members
1. Every member, whether regular or honorary, has the responsibility to abide in this Constitution and Bylaws, and rules and regulations set forth by the GC-AA.
2. Every member must pay membership fees and annual fees in an amount determined by the majority vote of the GC-AA Executive Committee and Board. All dues payments shall be made to the treasurer and/or duly representative of the Association.
3. Every member must support and promote the thrusts and objectives of the GC-AA and the College.
4. Every member must participate actively in all Gordon College activities and GC-AA programs and projects.
5. Every member must exercise his/her rights and do his/her responsibilities as a GC-AA member.
6. Every member shall be entitled to exercise full membership rights, including the right to vote and hold office within the Association in accordance with the By-laws.
#### Disciplinary Action Against Members
The GC-AA Board of Trustees shall review and act upon all disputes regarding infractions of Association rules and regulations. The Trustees can form an Ad Hoc Committee that can render decisions in terminating a member from the Association.
The final decision of the said committee shall become final upon the approval of the GC-AA Board of Trustees at its regular monthly or special meeting. The GC-AA Board may overrule the Committee by a two-thirds (2/3) vote.

# CESU Brigada Eskwela
[Image: CESU Brigada Eskwela]
[Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/extension/) [RDCES](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/tag/rdces/) [Community Extension](https://gordoncollege.edu.ph/w3/category/community-extension/)
**Thursday, August 17, 2023**
The close connection between the Bayanihan spirit and Brigada Eskwela highlights their shared focus on community service and volunteerism. The Bayanihan spirit embodies the collective collaboration of a community to attain common objectives, while Brigada Eskwela serves as a community service program uniting volunteers to improve school environments.
Upon receiving Kalalake Elementary School’s request to join the National Schools Maintenance Week, the institution and the dedicated BSEd English major student volunteers effectively carried out the clean-up drive at Kalalake Elementary School.
